title: SPQR: A History of Ancient Rome
excerpt: Mary Beard's sweeping and revisionist history of ancient Rome, from its mythical foundation to the extension of citizenship in 212 CE, told through the lens of the people — not just the emperors and senators.
keyIdeas: Rome was not a static empire but a constantly evolving experiment in governance, citizenship, and identity
The Roman Republic was more fragile, violent, and contested than the idealized picture suggests
Roman history is best understood through the experiences of ordinary people, not just the elite
keyTakeaways: Roman citizenship was gradually extended from a small elite to encompass the entire empire
The Republic fell not because of external threats but because its institutions could not contain elite competition
Rome's success owed as much to its willingness to incorporate outsiders as to military might
Many of our assumptions about Rome come from Roman propaganda that we have absorbed uncritically
The Roman Empire was far more diverse and complex than traditional narratives suggest
whoShouldRead: Anyone wanting a modern, accessible one-volume history of Rome
Readers who enjoyed the History of Rome podcast by Mike Duncan
People interested in how empires work and why they fall
Students looking for an introduction that engages with scholarly debates
whoShouldSkip: Those wanting a traditional military-political narrative of battles and emperors
Readers who prefer straightforward chronological storytelling
People looking for detailed coverage of the late empire
